title: Fresh Start
html:
The path is still empty when Lucas spots a figure at the far end of it, a man standing with his back turned. Lucas hasn't passed a single person since he stepped outside and the sight is enough to make him pick up his pace. He closes the distance quickly.

"Uncle, can I ask you something?" he calls out.

The man turns, and what Lucas sees brings him to a dead stop. The face is covered in dried blood. One eye has been pushed out of the socket and sits at the wrong angle. The other side of the jaw is torn open, the skin pulled back far enough to show white bone underneath. The man begins moving toward him, slow and unhurried, and it takes Lucas a full second before his legs start working again.

"AHH!" He's already turned and running before the sound fully clears his throat, back down the path the way he came. He doesn't look behind him. He can hear the footsteps following, slow, nothing close to his pace, but still there. He hits the backdoor at a full sprint and crashes through it with a Bang! that shakes the whole frame, and he drives the lock closed before the door has even finished swinging.

He stands with his back against the door. Outside, the pounding starts: Thum! Thum! Even and patient, the same interval between each hit.

Lucas slides down until he's sitting on the floor. 'That was the vegetable lady's husband.' He knows the face. He's sold fish at the market for years, walked past that stall dozens of times. He sits with the information for a moment and doesn't try to do anything with it.

He has dealt with dead fish and anything that didn't stop moving on the first spear hit. His parents died in the fish market accident when he was 10 but he was shielded from the result of that, and whoever made that decision was probably right. The vegetable vendor's husband is the most destroyed face Lucas has seen on anything still moving. There's a significant distance between that and everything else he has handled before.

The Thum! Thum! outside continues at the same pace.

"Is that a zombie," he says, to no one in particular. The word sits in the room and sounds a little absurd, which doesn't change what the face looked like. He watches his hand shaking on the floor beside him and decides it's involuntary and he isn't going to do anything about it. The practical question is what to do with the thing on the other side of the door, because it knows where he lives now and it isn't leaving.

He pushes himself up and goes to the kitchen. He opens the knife drawer, looks at what's in it, and pushes it closed. A knife means getting very close to the face he just saw. He goes to his room instead and pulls the bat from under the bed, and then he walks back to the kitchen and stands at the backdoor and waits.

"Click. Click." The handle moves without him touching it and the door swings inward. The zombie fills the frame, one eye aimed at Lucas and the other drifting past his shoulder at nothing, and Lucas swings down as hard as he can. The bat connects with a Bang! across the upper skull and the zombie staggers sideways into the doorframe, hard enough that the frame creaks, but it doesn't go down. He swings again across the same spot and it drops to one knee. He doesn't stop there. He keeps driving forward, one swing following the last, until the zombie is flat on the floor, and then he keeps going a little past that, until his arms are burning and the system chimes through.

---

[Level 1 Zombie Killed, Awarding 50 Exp!]

[Leveling Exp Reach: 50/50!]

[Leveling Up: Level 2!]

[Receiving 2 Stats Points and 1 Skills Point!]

---

Lucas crouches beside the body. His hands are still shaking. He looks at what's left of the vegetable vendor's husband — the face he's seen at the market dozens of times — and exhales. "Today is not going well," he mutters, and gets back to his feet.

He spends the better part of an hour cleaning. He fills a bucket, mops the floor in sections, and bags up everything he can't mop. He drags the body out to the bank by himself, which takes longer than he'd like, and tips it and the bags both into the lake. The polluted water closes over them without much disturbance. He stands at the bank for a moment and watches the surface settle back to flat.

"Can a zombie swim," he wonders aloud. Nothing comes up to answer.

He goes back inside, showers, and changes. He lies down on the bed still holding the bat and closes his eyes. He doesn't intend to fall asleep. His body makes that decision for him before his thoughts get very far.

***

He wakes at 4:34 pm to flat afternoon light through the window. He stares at the ceiling. The tremor in his hands has mostly settled. He turns his head and looks at the bat on the mattress beside him, then looks back at the ceiling.

The situation is reasonably clear when he works through it. The house is useful right now, but it won't stay that way. His food stores won't last more than a few days. The lake has mutated fish in it that grow by fighting each other, which means whatever comes out of there in a week will be larger than the 40-centimeter one he handled yesterday morning. If the vegetable vendor's husband was a zombie, there are more of them in the village, and at some point enough of them will find the house that the backdoor lock stops being a real solution. Staying put is just a longer timeline to the same outcome.

He also notes, in a more practical way, that the zombie paid 50 EXP against the fish's 10. He hasn't allocated his stat points yet and his skills list is still empty, which means he's operating well below whatever his ceiling ends up being. Outside is the only place that changes any of that.

He goes to the closet and finds the backpack his employer gave him at 14, still folded at the bottom with the tags on it. He spreads it on the floor and thinks about what to take.

The phone goes in first. The power grid has been down since morning and calls and data are dead, but the phone still runs a flashlight and he doesn't know what else might become useful. He wraps the power bank and charger in a shirt and packs those underneath it. He adds a pen and a small notebook, because the system doesn't appear to keep written records of anything and he'd rather have his own. He tucks the roll of bandage tape he found in the bathroom behind the soap into the front pocket.

The bat goes across the top of the bag where the handle is easy to reach. He'll carry the spear.

He doesn't pack much food because there isn't much left. That's a problem to solve outside.

He closes the bag at 7:09 pm and sets it against the wall and then sits down on the floor next to it. He'd intended to leave before dark, but the dark arrived while he was still going through the bathroom cabinet, and starting something new in the middle of the night in a village that already has zombies in it seems like poor timing. He puts the departure off until morning.

He turns to the rest of the house instead and goes through it properly, pulling open every drawer and checking every shelf he might have skipped on the first pass. He finds nothing useful he hasn't already packed. He finishes around 10 pm, sets the bag and spear on the floor beside the bed, and goes to sleep.

He doesn't think to check the backdoor. The zombie broke the lock when it forced its way through that afternoon, and the door now sits in the frame without actually catching. From inside, it looks like any other closed door. From outside, one firm push opens it.

***

January 22, 2029

Lucas wakes in the grey before sunrise. He dresses in the dim light without turning the lamp on, slings the bag over one shoulder, and picks up the spear with his right hand. He opens the backdoor and scans both directions along the bank: nothing moving, water flat, the lake quiet. He steps outside and walks a few paces from the house before turning around to face it.

The house is small and weathered, the wood darkened by years of lake humidity. In rice country, the belief is that a dwelling carries its own quiet presence, something that stays with the structure and turns harm away at the threshold. Lucas doesn't think about this on most days. He respected it because his parents did, and after they died the house kept standing, so he never had a reason to revisit the question.

He bows, low and fully held, once for his parents. Then twice more, a beat between each, for the house and for the water and land it sits above, which fed him on the months when work didn't.

He straightens, turns his back on the house, and starts walking.

The village market is the most crowded place in the area on a normal morning. He doesn't know what it is now, but it has supplies in it and he needs supplies, and that's enough reason to start there.
